claims.claim: When thermal runaway was triggered at cell1 (H-cell1) with no protective materials, all four cells completed thermal runaway within 15.94 s, averaging 5.31 s per cell.
claims.quote: The entire module completed thermal runaway in 15.94 s, averaging 5.31 s/cell.

claims.claim: H-cell3 (thermal runaway triggered at cell3) exhibited shorter propagation time (15.04 s), higher peak thermal runaway temperature (971.4 °C), and greater PHRR (89.85 kW) and THR (7.91 MJ) compared to H-cell1.
claims.quote: H-cell3 exhibited shorter propagation time (15.04 s), higher peak thermal runaway temperature (971.4 °C), and greater PHRR (89.85 kW) and THR (7.91 MJ).

claims.claim: Using 1 mm SMEP alone (H-cell1-S-1) delayed thermal runaway propagation from cell1 to cell4 to a total interval of 133.42 s, but did not completely prevent propagation.
claims.quote: resulting a total cell1 to cell4 interval of 133.42 s. Using 1.5 mm SMEP (Fig. 10 a4-c4) further delayed propagation... Thus both 1 mm and 1.5 mm SMEP effectively delayed thermal runaway propagation compared to the unprotected case, but neither thickness completely prevented thermal runaway propagation.

claims.claim: A standalone 3 mm KGSA barrier (H-cell1-K-3) completely blocked thermal runaway propagation when triggered at cell1, with cell3 and cell4 surface temperatures peaking at only 75.8 °C and 46.5 °C respectively.
claims.quote: the thicker KGSA blocked most heat such that 2-F rose only to 313.2 °C by 606.30 s with no further increase and flame spread remained confined; heat transfer to cell3 and cell4 was much slower, with their surface temperatures peaking at only 75.8 °C (1921.60 s) and 46.5 °C (2788.04 s), respectively, indicating that the effective thermal barrier provided by the thicker KGSA prevented thermal runaway in cells 3 and 4.

claims.claim: The combination of 2 mm KGSA and 1.5 mm SMEP (H-cell2-KS-1.5 and H-cell3-KS-1.5) successfully prevented thermal runaway propagation under non-first-cell triggering conditions, achieving a minimum surface temperature of only 55.6 °C.
claims.quote: H-cell2-KS-1.5 and H-cell3-KS-1.5 not only reduced PHRR and THR values but also achieved a minimum surface temperature of only 55.6 °C, with neither cell experiencing thermal runaway propagation.

claims.claim: Although SMEP alone reduced PHRR, it increased total heat release (THR) by 50.00% (1 mm) and 110.55% (1.5 mm) compared to H-cell1, due to the PA content in SMEP enhancing combustion.
claims.quote: Whereas although S-1 and S-1.5 lower the PHRR their THR increases by 50.00 % and 110.55 % due to the PA content in SMEP that enhances combustion and raises THR.

claims.claim: Bidirectional thermal runaway propagation (H-cell3) significantly promoted heat generation, resulting in THR of 7.91 MJ versus 2.56 MJ for unidirectional propagation (H-cell1).
claims.quote: H-cell3 exhibited two similar HRR peaks (PHRR = 89.85 kW, THR = 7.91 MJ), attributable to the heating plate at the left of cell3 and resulting bidirectional propagation, indicating that the trigger location affects the heat distribution in the battery module, and bidirectional propagation significantly promotes heat generation during battery thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The combination of 2 mm KGSA and 1 mm SMEP (H-cell2-KS-1 and H-cell3-KS-1) delayed but did not fully suppress thermal runaway propagation under non-first-cell triggering, with propagation intervals of 752.10 s and 664.12 s respectively.
claims.quote: H-cell2-KS-1 and H-cell3-KS-1 effectively reduced PHRR and peak temperature, significantly delaying thermal runaway propagation time (752.10 s/664.12 s). However, due to excessive thermal shock intensity, they could not completely prevent thermal runaway spread, resulting in high heat release.

claims.claim: This study only investigated thermal runaway propagation in open environments, and the effectiveness of KGSA and SMEP in suppressing thermal runaway propagation in sealed environments requires further exploration.
claims.quote: However, this work only investigated the propagation characteristics of thermal runaway in battery packs under open environments. The effectiveness of KGSA and SMEP in suppressing thermal runaway propagation in sealed environments requires further exploration in future research.

claims.claim: With increasing IFR content in SMEP, the melting enthalpy gradually decreased from 115.78 J/g (SMEP1) to 102.67 J/g (SMEP4), while self-extinguishing combustion duration shortened from 150 s to 3 s.
claims.quote: the melting enthalpies of SMEP1–4 are 115.78 J/g, 111.14 J/g, 104.75 J/g, and 102.67 J/g, respectively... The combustion durations for SMEP1-SMEP4 were 150 s, 71 s, 60 s, and 3 s, respectively.

claims.claim: In the battery module without PCM, all cells underwent thermal runaway successively within 2 minutes under the influence of the trigger cell, with the peak temperature of cell 5 reaching 870.95 K and peak temperatures of other cells between 743 and 773 K.
claims.quote: Under the influence of the trigger cell, all the cells undergo TR successively within 2 min. The peak temperature of cell 5 reaches 870.95 K. The peak temperatures of the other cells are between 743 and 773 K.

claims.claim: In the battery module with PCM, the peak temperature of the trigger cell was significantly reduced to 635.54 K compared to 870.95 K without PCM, and thermal runaway chain reactions did not propagate to other cells.
claims.quote: Although cell 5 still undergoes TR, the peak temperature is significantly reduced to 635.54 K, and the temperature drops quickly. The TR process of the trigger cell is significantly suppressed... cell 5 does not seriously affect the temperatures of other cells, and the chain reactions of TR no longer occur in the battery module.

claims.claim: When PCM thermal conductivity increases from 0.28 to 12.0 W/(m·K), the maximum cell temperature during thermal runaway decreases from 704.26 K to 417.49 K, successively inhibiting the electrolyte decomposition reaction (above 473.15 K) and positive active material-solvent reaction (above 442.15 K).
claims.quote: when kp increases from 0.28 to 12.0 W/(m⋅K), Tmax decreases from 704.26 K to 417.49 K; thus, the electrolyte decomposition reaction and positive active material-solvent reaction are inhibited successively, and the heat generation is reduced.

claims.claim: When PCM thickness increases from 2 to 5 mm, T2min and QTR decrease significantly; however, when PCM thickness reaches 6 mm or greater, T2min and QTR no longer change significantly because the PCM is sufficient to completely absorb the heat released by the cell during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: when d increases from 2 to 5 mm, T2min decreases significantly. When d ≥ 6 mm, T2min hardly changes. This phenomenon occurs because the PCM is insufficient to completely absorb the heat released by the cell during TR when its thickness is between 2 and 5 mm... When d ≥ 6 mm, the heat released by the cell during TR can be completely absorbed by the PCM.

claims.claim: PCM thickness has little effect on the maximum cell temperature during thermal runaway because TR occurs in a very short time after penetration and the amount of PCM participating in heat absorption before the cell reaches maximum temperature is approximately unchanged regardless of thickness.
claims.quote: The thickness of the PCM has little effect on Tmax. The reason is that TR occurs in a very short time after the cell is penetrated. Subject to its low thermal conductivity, the amount of the PCM participating in the heat absorption before the cell reaches its maximum temperature is approximately unchanged, even if the thicknesses of the PCM are different.

claims.claim: Without effective suppression measures, all cells in the LIB array underwent thermal runaway within 5 seconds, with TR propagating from the first to the last cell in that interval.
claims.quote: The TR propagated rapidly in the LIB array without suppression measures, and all cells underwent TR within 5 s.

claims.claim: The TR propagation interval (optimum suppression stage) lasted only approximately 5 seconds (Δt = 5 ± 1 s).
claims.quote: The duration of the optimum suppression stage was very short, only about 5 s (Δt = 5 ± 1 s).

claims.claim: Intermittent spray with a period of 5 s (DC = 0.5) failed to prevent or delay thermal runaway propagation due to insufficient average cooling power during the suppression stage.
claims.quote: The cooling capacity of intermittent spray with a period of 5 s during TR interval was insufficient, which cannot prevent or delay thermal runaway propagation.

claims.claim: Continuous water spray with an effective spray time of 20 s could delay but not prevent TR propagation, with TR propagation average time of 197 s.
claims.quote: Although TR propagation in the LIB array was not prevented, continuous water spray exhibited an excellent cooling effect during the application, delaying TR propagation and eliminating the risk of TR propagation in the initial stage.

claims.claim: Intermittent spray with DC of 0.1 (Pt = 20 s) had the lowest water consumption and average cooling power during the optimal suppression stage and had no effect on suppressing TR propagation, with LIB array propagation time of 7 s.
claims.quote: intermittent spray with DC of 0.1 possessed the lowest water consumption and average cooling power during the optimal suppression stage, which had no effect on suppressing TR propagation. The LIB array propagation time was 7 s
